does: This paper identifies and formalizes the error-regret mismatch problem in reward learning for reinforcement learning. The authors prove that while sufficiently low test error on a reward model theoretically guarantees low regret, for any fixed level of test error there exist realistic data distributions where this mismatch occurs. They demonstrate that distributional shift during policy optimization is the primary mechanism causing this phenomenon, meaning a reward model can achieve low training error on an offline dataset yet produce policies with high regret when deployed. The paper further shows that common policy regularization techniques used in methods like RLHF do not resolve this fundamental issue, providing both theoretical analysis and concrete examples of the mismatch.
connects: This work builds on the extensive literature on reward learning and inverse reinforcement learning that emerged from the challenge of reward specification in RL. It connects to recent practical methods like RLHF that attempt to learn human preferences, while grounding analysis in theoretical frameworks from distributional shift and domain adaptation. The paper extends prior work on offline RL and the theory of policy optimization under learned models by specifically investigating how errors in the reward function propagate through to final policy performance. It responds to optimism in the reward learning community that treating reward learning as a supervised learning problem is sufficient, questioning assumptions that low in-distribution error translates to good RL performance.
enables: This work opens directions for developing improved reward learning methods that explicitly account for distributional shift and policy optimization dynamics. It motivates research into alternative metrics for evaluating learned reward functions beyond standard supervised learning error, potentially including regret-based or robustness-based measures. The theoretical framework could inspire new approaches to reward learning that provide guarantees under distribution shift, such as those based on uncertainty quantification or conservative estimation. The results also highlight the need for better benchmark and evaluation practices in reward learning research, pointing toward empirical studies that measure the alignment between reward model accuracy and actual policy performance across diverse settings rather than relying solely on supervised learning metrics.
